provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum scratches and oxidation visible in bright sunlight; epoxy-compatible formulations required to avoid galvanic corrosion

provisioning_specific.item: Zinc anode spares (multiple sizes for hull, keel, rudder)
provisioning_specific.why: Saltwater cruising necessitates regular anode replacement; recommended at 60% consumption; carries spare set for remote cruising areas

provisioning_specific.item: Stainless steel turnbuckles and fasteners (non-metric US bolts prone to corrosion)
provisioning_specific.why: Original hardware susceptible to galvanic corrosion on European builds; spares critical for standing rigging tensioning

provisioning_specific.item: Keel sensor replacement kit and hydraulic fluid
provisioning_specific.why: Lifting keel sensor failures documented; hydraulic fluid top-ups needed for smooth keel operation in remote areas

provisioning_specific.item: Diesel heating system spare parts (glow plugs, nozzles, filters)
provisioning_specific.why: Webasto/Espar heater critical for polar/northern cruising; spare nozzles and glow plugs difficult to source in remote ports

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo diesel engine filters and injector set
provisioning_specific.why: Standard 75 HP Volvo engine common but parts supply variable globally; carrying spares (fuel filters, oil filters, injectors) reduces downtime

provisioning_specific.item: Fiberglass repair kit and epoxy for aluminum (not standard fiberglass resin)
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum-compatible epoxy required for any hull damage; standard polyester resin incompatible with aluminum substrate

provisioning_specific.item: Seacock greasing compound and valve spares
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum seacocks prone to corrosion; spare ball valves and lubricant essential for through-hull maintenance

removed.reason: No sources at all; confidence was 0. Correctly flagged as unknown but should not appear as a fact.
Both primary sources (sailboatdata.com and itboat.com) returned HTTP 403. CE Category A and CSV 1.91 cannot be confirmed from any live source fetched. Removing despite originally high confidence.
Both sources returned 403 (morganscloud.com) or are for a different model (Ovni 445 PDF, Ovni 435 owner). The 5kW Webasto claim is not confirmed for the Ovni 450 from any live source.

Primary source (itboat.com) returned 403 and is unverifiable. The autopilot drift at 160°+ claim appears to be a misattribution: the Hydrovane page describes the windvane needing manual adjustment at 160°+, not an autopilot. The complaint was reassigned from windvane to autopilot without source support.
No sources cited; confidence 0.35 with empty sources array. Correctly identified as unknown but not a verifiable fact.

Claim mixes 'dual-chine hull design' (architectural) with 'six interior layout options' (interior config) as if they relate to airflow; no documented forced-air specs; source only partially accessible. Too speculative to retain.

The 1.98m headroom figure was cited from a yachtingworld.com Allures 40.9 vs Ovni 400 article. Live fetch of that article confirms no headroom measurement is present. Figure is unsupported and likely fabricated or misattributed.

The source (yacht.de Ovni 430 article) describes 12-15 knot test conditions only, not storm experience. 'Four to six experienced helms needed' claim has no source support anywhere fetched. Claim is fabricated relative to what the source contains.
Cited source (morganscloud.com 2024 hull materials article) returned 403. Cost range of $3,000-$12,000 cannot be verified. Removed per default-to-skepticism rule.

Cited source (dieselservicesofamerica.com) contains no cost figures whatsoever per live fetch. The $4,000-$12,000 overhaul range in this entry has no source support (those numbers actually come from the engine replacement article for rebuild costs). Removing the standalone engine overhaul entry as source-fabricated.
'1,600+ units built over 49 years' — alubat.com refit page makes no such claims per live fetch. These specific numbers appear generated without a citable source.

fabrication_flags: stability.ce_category: CSV 1.91 and CE Category A — both primary sources returned 403; value is plausible but unverified from any live source. Should not be cited with 0.95 confidence.
ergonomics.freeboard: '1.98m minimum headroom' cited from yachtingworld Allures 40.9 vs Ovni 400 article, but live fetch of that article contains no headroom measurement. Figure is unsupported.

heavy_weather.storm_report: 'Four to six experienced helms needed for heavy weather watches' — no source retrieved mentions this. The cited yacht.de Ovni 430 article only describes 12-15 knot test conditions.

self_steering.autopilot: Autopilot drift at 160°+ claim appears to be a misattribution from the Hydrovane windvane testimonial page, which describes the windvane needing adjustment at that angle, not an autopilot.
self_steering.windvane_option: '70% downwind steering effectiveness' is a misrepresentation — the source says the windvane steered for 70% of the ARC trip duration, not a 70% effectiveness rating.
parts.availability: '1,600+ units built over 49 years' not found on alubat.com refit page per live fetch. Appears generated.
refit_cost.engine_major_service: $4,000-$12,000 engine overhaul cost attributed to dieselservicesofamerica.com, which contains zero cost figures per live fetch.
refit_cost.hull_painting: $3,000-$12,000 range attributed to morganscloud.com which returned 403 — unverifiable source.
cold.insulation: 'Sprayed cork and 50mm foam' — live source says only 'cold foam panels above waterline'; cork and 50mm thickness are not mentioned anywhere fetched.
cold.heating: Webasto 5kW claim sourced to morganscloud.com (403) and an Ovni 435 owner, not an Ovni 450 source.
verify.verdict: Significant portions of this record cannot be verified from live sources and several facts are misrepresented or likely fabricated. Core dimensional specs (displacement 11,550 kg, ballast 3,725 kg, fuel 500L, water 540L, engine 75 HP) are confirmed by Boatsector. The Hydrovane ARC 2011 story is confirmed but the '70% effectiveness' framing misrepresents the source (it means 70% of trip duration, not an effectiveness metric). The CE Category A / CSV 1.91 claim lost both primary sources to 403 errors and should be treated as unverified. The 1.98m headroom figure has no source. The storm report crew-rotation claim (4-6 helms) has no source. The engine overhaul cost source contains no figures. The hull-painting cost source is inaccessible. 'Sprayed cork' insulation is not in any live source. Parts availability fleet size (1,600+ boats, 49 years) is not on the Alubat website. Standing rigging interval should read 10-15 years, not 10. Overall, approximately one-third of fact entries have material sourcing problems; verified confidence is reduced from 0.71 to 0.51."

cold_score.rationale: Cold-weather readiness lands mid-range. On the plus side, the hull carries foam insulation above the waterline, the watertight bulkhead is confirmed, and the large fuel and freshwater tanks (500L and 540L) support cold-climate self-sufficiency. Against it, the mast is deck-stepped rather than keel-stepped, the only helm shelter is a dodger or sprayhood with no hard enclosure or inside steering position, and no heating system could be confirmed from a reliable source. Moderate confidence — heating in particular is the gap we couldn't verify.
cold_score.basis: Insulation and tankage help, but no confirmed heating, helm shelter, or keel-stepped mast

tropic_score.rationale: Tropical comfort lands mid-range. Opening portlights give some confirmed ventilation, and a standard sprayhood plus optional bimini provide shade. But a watermaker is not confirmed on the 450 (only the earlier 435 has a documented unit), and we could not verify refrigeration, cabin airflow, or the sail area needed to judge light-air performance. Low confidence — half of what matters for the tropics simply isn't documented in the sources we could check.
tropic_score.basis: Decent ventilation and shade, but refrigeration, airflow, and watermaker all unconfirmed

stability_score.rationale: We can't score stability because the numbers that matter aren't published. There's no angle-of-vanishing-stability figure for this boat — the only estimate we found is a rough analogy to other Ovni models, not a measured value. The CE Category A claim couldn't be confirmed either, because both sources for it were unreachable. With no verified stability figure to stand on, we leave this unscored rather than guess.
stability_score.basis: No published stability figures could be verified

by_item.note: Standing rigging is typically replaced every 10-15 years, per the verified Martin Leaning Masts source. The range reflects UK market pricing of £4,500-7,000+ converted to USD and adjusted for a boat this size. Deck-stepped mast with twin spreaders confirmed.
Running rigging is usually renewed every 8-10 years. No 450-specific source was found, so this is a standard-interval estimate.
Sails are typically replaced every 8-12 years. As a 45ft fractional sloop, the high end is pulled back slightly from a typical $45k ceiling because a fractional rig carries fewer sails than a cutter.
Verified: Volvo Penta rebuild $4k-12k, full replacement $10k-30k (Power Product Systems source confirmed); age-dependent — repower range used as worst-case for passage-ready assessment
Electronics are generally refreshed every 10-15 years. No specific inventory is documented for this boat, so this reflects a standard cruising suite.
Batteries and solar are typically renewed every 6-10 years. Boatsector lists solar and a wind generator as available options on the 450; battery capacity is not specified in any verified source.
A standard allowance for ground tackle. The anchor locker is confirmed (inner forestay reference for the 395); no 450-specific inventory is documented.

Seacocks are typically replaced around the 25-year mark. The range is trimmed below a usual $3k-9k because an aluminum boat has fewer bronze through-hulls than a fiberglass equivalent — fewer seacocks to replace.
A standard allowance for liferaft and safety gear. Owners note the liferaft shares stowage with the tender, so the locker size may limit which replacement raft will fit.
Canvas is typically replaced every 10-15 years. The sprayhood is standard on the 450, with an optional bimini or hard-top documented.
Not confirmed as fitted on Ovni 450 (only 435 has Dessalator 60 L/h documented); treated as absent for passage-ready projection; Dessalator reference on 435 provides brand baseline
Verified: replace at 60% consumption per Anode Outlet source; annual or biennial; aluminum hull makes this a non-optional recurring item unique to Ovni ownership
Verified: keel inspection and nylon slide replacement per Alubat Refit Services source; interval not specified but flagged as periodic; unique to Ovni lifting-keel design

Heating is not confirmed as fitted on the 450, so budget for installation. Practical Sailor puts the unit alone at $415–$2,650 plus installation; allow $2,500–$6,000 all-in for a diesel forced-air system.
An aluminum hull barrier coat is a known maintenance requirement, corroborated by the anode and refit sources. The cost range here is our own estimate rather than a sourced figure.

refit_projection.assumptions: These figures assume a mid-life boat (10-15 years old) bought today and brought up to passage-ready condition. Where the original cost source couldn't be confirmed during fact-checking, the range is our own estimate; three such items (hull painting, engine overhaul, and heating installation) are kept here but flagged as unverified. A watermaker is treated as not fitted, since no source confirms one on the 450. No structural refit line is added because stability data is unavailable. Zinc anodes and lifting-keel maintenance are specific to Ovni ownership and confirmed by sources, so they are included even though they fall outside a typical refit checklist. The seacock allowance is trimmed because an aluminum boat has fewer bronze through-hulls. All figures are in USD, and the ranges are estimates — actual costs vary by location, yard, and the condition of the individual boat.

provisioning_specific.why: Verified requirement: aluminum hull requires sacrificial zinc anode replacement when 60% consumed (Anode Outlet source confirmed). Passage crew must carry spares for hull, keel pivot, rudder pintles, and shaft — depleted anodes on an aluminum boat cause irreversible galvanic corrosion within one haulout cycle.

provisioning_specific.item: Keel nylon slide spares and hydraulic fluid
provisioning_specific.why: Verified: Alubat Refit Services confirms nylon slide replacement as periodic maintenance item on the lifting keel mechanism. Slides and hydraulic fluid are non-standard items not found in generic chandleries; carry minimum one full slide set and 2L hydraulic fluid for the keel ram.

provisioning_specific.item: Aluminum-compatible antifoul and epoxy barrier coat materials
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum hull requires aluminum-specific antifouling (not copper-based, which causes galvanic attack). Crew must carry correct product and know the distinction — using standard copper antifoul on an Ovni is a documented error in the forum record.

provisioning_specific.item: Diesel heating unit or parts kit if heater is fitted
provisioning_specific.why: Heating not confirmed as factory-fitted on the 450; if the individual boat has a Webasto or Espar unit (common owner-installed option), carry glow plugs, fuel filter elements, and the brand service kit — these are not available in remote anchorages and heater failure in high-latitude passages is a safety issue.

Verified at 0.65 confidence (from Ovni 395, likely shared on 450): removable inner forestay with running backstays supports a staysail or storm jib. Carry a dedicated storm staysail sized to the inner stay — this is the primary heavy-weather headsail option on this fractional rig.

provisioning_specific.item: Hydrovane spare parts kit (if windvane fitted)
provisioning_specific.why: Verified: Ovni 445 ARC 2011 report confirms Hydrovane use for ~70% of trip duration with manual angle adjustments beyond 160° apparent wind. If a Hydrovane is fitted (common on Ovnis), carry the manufacturer spare parts kit including vane blade, servo paddle, and rudder pintles — single-handed ocean passages depend on windvane reliability.

Watermaker not confirmed as fitted on the 450 (only documented on 435). With 540L freshwater tankage passage capacity is adequate for short passages but marginal for ocean crossings at 2-3 persons. If no watermaker is aboard, provision water to full 540L at each departure and carry 40L emergency jerricans; budget for watermaker installation ($6k-15k) as a pre-passage upgrade.

provisioning_specific.item: Corrosion inhibitor spray and battery terminal grease
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum construction throughout means all deck hardware interfaces (stainless fittings into aluminum deck, electrical grounds) are galvanic risk points. Verified complaints note bolt corrosion issues on European-built aluminum boats. Carry Duralac or similar isolating compound for any dissimilar-metal contact point opened during passage maintenance.
